question: What is the socio-cultural background of the work?
What is the historical context in which the work has been written?
What are the main historical events from the time the author of the work you are reading lived?
What are the main events in the life of the author you are studying?
Can an author write successfully about a culture of which they are not a part of?
How is the cultural context of the work you are studying at the moment revealed?
Was this your experience with the work so far?
What is your experience with the work so far?
Have you heard about this author till now?
Have you heard about this book till now? In which context/circumstance?
Do you consider the work you are analysing now as effective in creating a new world in your mind? Why? What contributes to the creation of that world?
How does a work make its reader creative when it comes to exploring the possible different meanings of that work?
How many possible interpretations of the work are you reading or which of its elements can you find? Give me at least 2 examples of such interpretations.
Write an alternative ending or part of the book you are reading that changes its interpretation, but that tries to replicate the author's style.
Was this book difficult for you to understand?
Did you have to do research to fully understand this book?
Was this book revealing another meaning after a second reading?
What is the main aim of the work that you are studying? How do you know this?
Why do you think the author wrote this work?
How accessible is this work to you as a reader?
What might prevent a reader understanding this work?
Which character from this book do you really admire?
How far do you think it was the intention of the work to elicit this response in you?
Which are the main, secondary and episodic characters of this book?
Which characters represent some aspect of the author's identity?
Which characters do you find similar to your personality?
Which characters do you identify with?
To what extent have you identified with or felt challenged by the perspectives offered in the book?
Which issue has this book confirmed or deepened your views on?
How has this book confirmed or deepened your views?
How successfully has the book presented a particular point of view?
Which is the point of view presented by this book?
How similar is the world in the work you are reading to the real world?
How recognizable are the characters and their attitudes?
How much are the characters and their attitudes similar to real people?
In what ways does the use of language in the works you have read represent in itself a view of the world?
How has this book impacted the way you think about writing?
Which is the main topic of this book?
How has this book impacted the way you think about this topic?
How has this book impacted the way you think about reading?
What was it about the work that transformed your thinking?
What work from the ones you read till now has changed your thinking? What is being transformed: your views on reading, on writing or on an issue?
Compare two works that you have read in the course. How does comparing them change how you understand each work? How do the works seem different after this comparison?
What is the central idea or message of this work?
Which moments or passages affected you most, and why?
How has your understanding of the text changed since your first reading?
What were your first impressions of the text?
What questions did the text raise for you?
What possible interpretations can you already identify?
What do you need to research further?
Which quotations best support your interpretation?
How does this quotation connect to a theme, character, or stylistic feature?
What feedback have you received and how will you apply it?
What new skills or knowledge did you develop while studying this work?
What was difficult for you and how did you overcome it?
What strategies helped you improve your analysis or writing?
Which concept is most relevant to this work and why?
How does the text construct identity or represent culture?
How does the author use perspective to influence the reader?
Which literary devices are most important in this extract (e.g., symbolism, irony, narrative voice)?
How do these techniques contribute to meaning?
Can you explain this term clearly in your own words?
What exactly is the question asking you to do?
What evidence supports your analysis or evaluation?
How will you structure your response to meet the command term?
What is your main argument (thesis) about the text?
How do structure, language, and style support this argument?
Are there alternative interpretations you should address?
What global issue does this text engage with (power, inequality, identity, conflict, environment)?
How is this issue presented through characters or events?
Why is this issue relevant today?
What specific questions do you want feedback on?
Which part of your analysis are you least confident about?
How can you improve your argument based on your tutor's comments?
What was your biggest challenge in this task?
What progress are you most proud of?
What will you focus on improving next?
What social, historical, or cultural context influenced the author?
How might these conditions shape the characters and themes?
How would the text change if written in a different time period?
How might readers from different eras interpret this text differently?
What modern perspectives change the way we read this work today?
Why might this text remain relevant?
What would change if this text were written in another genre or medium?
How would meaning shift if the narrator or perspective changed?
What stylistic elements are essential to preserve the message?
How can you reinterpret the text creatively (monologue, diary entry, alternative ending)?
What themes or characters will you highlight in your response?
How does your creative response stay faithful to the original text?
How does language reflect social or cultural power in the text?
What cultural assumptions are present?
How does the author use language to shape meaning and tone?
How might the original audience have understood this text differently from modern readers?
Which historical or social changes could alter the interpretation of this work?
What themes might become more important today than at the time of publication?
How would readers from different generations respond emotionally or ethically to this text?
Why do you think this work continues (or does not continue) to be read and discussed?
What would change if this text were rewritten in another form (play → short story, novel → poem, prose → script)?
How would meaning shift if you changed the narrator, tense, or point of view?
What happens if the setting is modernized or relocated?
Which elements must remain unchanged to preserve the author's core message?
What transformation would best highlight the central theme?
Which character, moment, or conflict do you want to explore creatively?
How can you show understanding of the original text through your creative response?
What tone and style will you adopt to match or contrast the author's voice?
How does your creative piece reflect the themes or issues of the original work?
What new perspective does your response offer?
How might readers from different cultures interpret the characters or values differently?
What political or social perspectives could influence modern interpretations?
Which elements might be controversial or misunderstood today?
How could language barriers or translation affect meaning?
How would readers from marginalized or dominant groups respond differently?
What themes are shared between the two works? How are they treated differently?
How do the authors' stylistic choices shape meaning in each text?
What similarities or differences exist in narrative voice or perspective?
How does context influence the messages of each work?
How does the content and structure of the work make this character stand out among the others in the book?
What is the purpose of studying literature and what do we gain from it?
What skills, knowledge, or perspectives do you develop by studying this text?
How does analyzing language, structure, and context help you understand the text more deeply?
How do literary texts influence readers emotionally and intellectually?
How did this text affect your thoughts, emotions, or values?
How might different readers react differently to this same text?
How is meaning created and understood in a literary text?
What techniques does the author use to create meaning (language, imagery, symbolism, narration)?
How can readers interpret the same text in different ways?
How does language change depending on the type of text?
What language features are typical of this literary form (novel, play, poem, graphic text)?
How do these language choices shape the reader's experience?
How do the way a text is built and written influence its message?
How does the organization of the text (chapters, scenes, chronology) guide understanding?
How does the author's stylistic voice (tone, register, sentence structure) influence interpretation?
How do texts help us understand the world while also questioning our beliefs?
What new ideas, perspectives, or social issues does the text help you understand?
What aspects of the text challenge your assumptions, values, or comfort zone?
Do a poem and an informational text have different values to you as a reader?
Which text is more effective in communicating its ideas — and why?
What emotions or thoughts did this text make you feel or have?
How did this text change the way you think about real-world or global issues?
Did this text help you understand a character, situation, or problem differently? How?
Did the story make you reflect on issues outside the text (for example society, relationships, power, or identity)?
In what ways can this text be both enjoyable to read and useful for academic analysis?
What did you personally learn from reading this work?
How is reading a poem or story different from reading a news article about the same topic?
What does literature help you understand that factual texts may not?
What emotions or ideas does a literary text create compared to an informational text?
What special skills do you need to read and analyze a poem or novel (for example, noticing imagery or symbolism)?
Why might a literary text be valuable even if it does not give direct facts?
What personal value does this text have for you as a reader?
How do the form and structure of the text (for example layout, chapters, scenes, or patterns) help create meaning?
How do the author's language choices (imagery, word choice, symbols) shape what the text communicates?
Can the same text be understood in different ways? Why?
What is your own interpretation of this text? What evidence supports it?
How might critics or readers from the past or present interpret this text differently?
Do you think form and meaning can be separated, or are they always connected? Explain your idea.
What are the typical features of this type of text (for example poem, play, novel, speech)?
Which stylistic or structural elements make this text clearly belong to this form?
How does the author use or adapt these conventions?
What differences do you notice when you compare this form to another literary form?
Has this type of text changed over time? If yes, how?
Do you think this form still follows traditional rules, or does it challenge them? Explain.
How is the text organized (chapters, scenes, timeline)?
How does this structure help develop ideas, characters, or tension?
Why do you think the author chose this structure instead of another one?
How does the narrator's voice or point of view influence your understanding of the story?
What language choices (tone, imagery, description) shape how you imagine the setting or characters?
How would the meaning change if the structure or style were different?
What new ideas or perspectives did this text help you understand?
Did you identify with any character or situation? Why?
Did this text make you question your beliefs or assumptions? How?
What parts of the text were difficult, uncomfortable, or challenging to read?
How did these challenges help you think more deeply about the topic?
What does this text help you understand about the world or about yourself?
What kinds of things can literature teach us about people, society, or the world?
Why do you think literature is still important today?
What knowledge does this text give you (emotional, cultural, historical, moral)?
How is this type of knowledge different from what you learn in science or history?
Can different readers understand the same text in different ways? Why?
Do you think there is one "correct" interpretation or many valid ones? Explain.
How does the author guide the reader's understanding through language and structure?
How does the reader's background influence how they understand the text?
What makes an interpretation strong or convincing?
Why is using evidence from the text important?
What differences do you notice between reading a text in the original language and in translation?
What meaning or effects might be lost or changed in translation?
Who do you think the text was originally written for?
What difficulties can modern readers face when reading this text?
How is reading a printed book different from reading online or watching a performance?
How does the format change your understanding or emotional response?
What types of evidence should you use to support your ideas (quotes, techniques, context)?
Why is explanation more important than just quoting?
Do literary forms (poems, plays, novels) restrict writers or help them create meaning?
How can authors break or adapt rules to create new effects?
What have you learned about literature by studying these texts?
Which ideas, themes, or messages from these works stayed with you most?
How could you use one of these works in a CAS project (for example reading promotion, workshops, performances, campaigns)?
Which CAS strand would this fit best: Creativity, Activity, or Service — and why?
Are these works available in your school or local library?
Are they accessible in other languages or formats?
Why might it be important to help others discover these texts or this author?
What social or global issues appear in this text?
How could you use this work to raise awareness about these issues?
Who would be the target audience for this project?
Could you adapt this text into a performance, reading event, or multimedia project?
What part of the text would be most powerful to present publicly?
What message would you want the audience to remember?
Is there injustice or inequality presented in the text?
Does this make you want to take action or support a cause? How?
What small, realistic action could you start with?
Why does the time and culture in which a text is written and read matter?
What historical or cultural factors influenced the author when writing this text?
How do these factors affect how readers understand the text today?
How should we approach texts that come from unfamiliar contexts?
What aspects of this text feel unfamiliar or different from your own culture or time?
What can you do to better understand these differences (research, context, discussion)?
How much can literature teach us about another culture?
What does this text reveal about the values, traditions, or social issues of its culture?
What might the text not show or represent fully?
How does a text's meaning or importance change as society changes?
How might readers in the past have understood this text differently from modern readers?
Which themes or ideas feel more important today, and why?
How do texts reflect and shape cultural traditions and behavior?
What cultural practices or social norms are shown in this text?
How might this text influence the way people think about these practices?
How does language show social groups and identities?
How does the author use language to show class, gender, power, or identity?
How do these language choices affect the reader's understanding of characters or society?
How important is knowing the background (time, place, culture) of a text for understanding its meaning?
How well can a modern reader understand a text that was written in a very different historical or cultural context?
What can readers from different cultures or time periods learn from a literary text?
Is what we learn about a culture from literature always accurate or reliable?
Can a literary text ever stop being relevant for certain readers or communities?
What reasons could be used to support this idea?
Is it fair or useful to analyze older texts using modern values and ideas?
What are the limits of doing this?
To what extent can literary texts be used as sources of historical information?
How can readers judge whether a translation is good quality?
Why might some translations be more effective than others?
How does the translator's time and cultural context influence the translation?
What problems or risks can occur when an author writes about a culture they do not belong to?
What can literature teach us about the beliefs, values, and traditions of a culture?
Can a literary text have a negative or harmful effect on its audience?
When, if ever, is censoring a literary text justified?
How does this text follow the usual rules of its literary form, and how does it break or change them?
How do literary rules and traditions change as society and culture change?
How can very different texts still share similar ideas, themes, or techniques?
What makes a literary text become a "classic"?
Do you think the idea of a "classic" is always fair or objective?
How can one text present different points of view about the same topic or theme?
How can comparing texts and interpreting them change the way you understand literature or the world?
Why might an author choose one literary form instead of another to communicate a specific message?
How does the chosen literary form help show character development or personal growth?
What effect does starting a story in the middle or end of a character's life have on the reader's understanding?
How does delayed revelation of information shape suspense or reader engagement?
What is the effect of using an unreliable narrator on the reader's trust and interpretation?
Why would an author intentionally create uncertainty for the reader?
How should readers deal with ambiguity or incomplete information in a text?
How do changing social values influence the way literary forms are adapted over time?
What similarities can you find between changes in literature and changes in other cultural forms (fashion, music, art)?
How do texts from the same literary form show gradual change across generations?
How can studying older and newer versions of the same genre help you understand literary evolution?
How can texts from very different cultures or time periods achieve the same overall purpose or message?
How can different literary techniques (imagery, dialogue, symbolism) lead to similar emotional or ideological effects?
What shared human experiences (for example love, loss, migration, identity) connect the texts you are studying?
How can different artistic methods produce similar reader responses?
What patterns of connection can you identify across your selected works?
Who decides which texts become "classics"?
What social, political, or cultural power structures influence which texts are valued?
Why might some authors or cultures be excluded from the literary canon?
How do definitions of "classic" differ across countries and cultures?
What personal criteria do you use to decide whether a text deserves classic status?
How do your reading choices reflect your understanding of literary value?
How does this text present different viewpoints on the same topic or conflict?
Which perspectives are given more importance or more space in the text? Why?
How do differences in age, gender, background, or identity shape the viewpoints shown?
Can opposing perspectives in the same text both be meaningful or valid? Why or why not?
What perspectives are missing or underrepresented in the text?
How does including multiple viewpoints deepen your understanding of the issue?
How has interpreting this text changed the way you think about its themes or characters?
How has comparing this text with another work changed your understanding of both texts?
How can reading inspire writers to reshape or recreate earlier texts?
How has rereading this text changed your interpretation over time?
How has engaging with this text influenced the way you see the world or your own position in it?
What new meanings have you created as a reader through interpretation?
Can reading literature help you develop ideas or knowledge that you did not have before? How?
What criteria do people use to judge whether a text has "literary value"?
Who has the authority to decide what counts as "good literature"?
How does placing a text into a genre or category influence how readers interpret it?
Can labeling a text limit or shape what readers notice or expect?
Does knowing literary rules and conventions make a text easier to understand, or can it sometimes confuse readers?
When might too much technical knowledge about literature reduce enjoyment or personal engagement?
What can you learn by comparing different translations of the same text?
How can adaptations (films, stage versions, rewrites) reveal new meanings in the original text?
Can transforming a text help clarify ideas that were unclear before?
How do reviews, critics, teachers, or classmates shape the way you understand a text?
Can popular opinion change the perceived meaning or value of a literary work?
What patterns or ideas become clearer when you read multiple works by the same author?
How can reading several texts by one writer deepen your understanding of their themes or style?
How does recognizing references to other texts change your understanding of a story?
What new insights can you gain by reading two texts that explore the same theme in different ways?
Which global issue is this book related to?
For the oral: In which ways the global issue of your choice is presented through the content and form of two of the works that you have studied?
